items.myth: Supervised learning means the algorithm watches a human in real time.
items.reality: Supervision refers to labeled training data — known y values — not live human oversight during each gradient step. Once labels are collected, training is automated.
items.example: MNIST digits are pre-labeled; SGD updates weights from (image, digit) pairs without a teacher present at each step.

items.myth: Supervised learning only applies to classification.
items.reality: Any problem with labeled (x, y) pairs is supervised — including regression (continuous y), ranking, and structured prediction.
items.example: Predicting house prices from past sales is supervised regression, not unsupervised, because each home has a known sale price label.

items.myth: More labeled data always fixes a bad model or wrong problem framing.
items.reality: Labels must match the deployment task, be reasonably accurate, and cover the input distribution. Bad labels or wrong y definition hurt regardless of volume.
items.example: Training on US housing prices to deploy in another country fails even with millions of labels if market dynamics differ.

items.myth: Unsupervised and supervised learning are mutually exclusive in practice.
items.reality: Pipelines often combine them: unsupervised pretraining on unlabeled data, then supervised fine-tuning on a smaller labeled set.
items.example: Pretrain a language model on raw text (self-supervised), then SFT on (prompt, response) pairs for a chatbot.
